title: Agent Harnesses and Runtime Systems
description: A comprehensive module covering the runtime layer that executes agent architectures — loop execution, tool dispatch, context management, state tracking, control flow, persistence, observability, and human intervention.

audience.primary: Intermediate AI engineers and platform developers building production agent systems

prerequisites: Can write Python functions and classes
Has called an LLM API at least once

learningObjectives.description: Define the harness layer and classify its eight operational concerns
Trace a single agent turn through the run/turn/step execution model
Implement a tool dispatch system with validation and error handling
Design context assembly strategies that respect token limits
Implement checkpoint-based persistence for crash recovery
Design human-in-the-loop intervention patterns for consequential actions
Build a functional agent harness from first principles
Evaluate production frameworks against the eight-concern framework

lessons.title: What Is an Agent Harness?
lessons.summary: Introduction to the harness layer, its eight operational concerns, and the spectrum from minimal to full-featured harnesses.

accessibility.altText: Diagram showing eight harness concerns: loop execution, tool dispatch, context management, state tracking, control flow, persistence, observability, and human intervention
accessibility.fallbackText: The harness layer manages eight concerns: loop execution, tool dispatch, context management, state tracking, control flow, persistence, observability, and human intervention.

recommendations.rationale: Visual overview of the eight harness concerns helps learners build a mental model.
recommendations.contentBrief: Diagram showing the eight operational concerns of an agent harness.
recommendations.accessibilityNotes: Provide fallback text listing all eight concerns.

metacognition.prompt: Before starting, rate your understanding of what happens between calling an LLM API and getting a tool execution result.
Which of the eight harness concerns do you think would be most challenging to implement correctly?

lessons.title: Agent Loop Internals
lessons.summary: Understanding the run/turn/step execution model, stop conditions, and common loop patterns.

recommendations.rationale: Flowchart of the run-turn-step model clarifies execution flow.
recommendations.contentBrief: Flowchart showing run → turn → step → model call → tool execution → next turn cycle.
recommendations.accessibilityNotes: Describe the cycle in text form.

metacognition.prompt: What do you want to understand about agent loops by the end of this lesson?

lessons.title: Tool Dispatch
lessons.summary: Tool registry patterns, validation strategies, execution mechanisms, and error handling for agent tools.

recommendations.rationale: Sequence diagram showing validation → execution → error handling flow.
recommendations.contentBrief: Sequence showing tool call validation, registry lookup, execution, and error handling.
recommendations.accessibilityNotes: Describe each step in the dispatch pipeline textually.

metacognition.prompt: What edge cases in tool dispatch could cause production failures?

lessons.title: Context, Goals, and State
lessons.summary: Context assembly strategies, goal tracking mechanisms, and working memory management within token limits.

recommendations.rationale: Compare context assembly strategies by token budget, latency, and complexity.
recommendations.contentBrief: Matrix comparing full history, sliding window, summarization, and semantic chunking strategies.
recommendations.accessibilityNotes: Use DataTable component with clear column headers.

metacognition.prompt: How confident are you in designing context windows that balance history and token limits?

lessons.title: Control Flow
lessons.summary: Control flow patterns: loops, graphs, handoffs, conditional routing, and graph-based execution.

recommendations.accessibilityNotes: Describe each pattern's structure in text.

lessons.title: Persistence and Checkpointing

lessons.title: Human-in-the-Loop
lessons.summary: Approval gates, escalation triggers, intervention patterns, and user confirmation for consequential actions.

lessons.title: Choosing a Harness + Frontier
lessons.summary: Decision framework for selecting a harness approach, MCP ecosystem integration, agent-to-agent communication, and frontier research appendix.

recommendations.rationale: Decision tree for choosing between custom harness, LangGraph, and Temporal.
recommendations.contentBrief: Decision tree with criteria for selecting harness approach based on project requirements.

metacognition.prompt: What harness approach would you choose for your current or upcoming project, and why?

concepts.definition: The harness concern controlling when the agent runs, when it stops, and how many steps it may take.
The harness concern that validates tool-call requests, routes them to implementations, handles errors, and formats results.
The harness concern that assembles message history, system prompts, and tool schemas each turn within token limits.
The harness concern tracking agent objectives, working memory, and progress indicators.
The harness concern routing execution through loops, branches, handoffs, or graph transitions.
The harness concern checkpointing state to durable storage for crash recovery and run resumption.
The harness concern emitting traces, tracking costs, supporting replay, and integrating with monitoring.
The harness concern defining approval gates, escalation triggers, and manual override mechanisms.

misconceptions.misconception: The harness is just a while loop around the LLM API.
misconceptions.correction: The harness manages eight distinct operational concerns beyond simple iteration: tool dispatch, context assembly, state tracking, control flow, persistence, observability, and human intervention.

misconceptions.misconception: Frameworks like LangGraph eliminate the need for custom harness code.
misconceptions.correction: Frameworks shift the abstraction level but you still implement domain-specific logic. They handle common concerns but require custom tool definitions, context strategies, and control flow design.

misconceptions.misconception: Observability is just adding print statements or log files.
misconceptions.correction: Production observability includes structured traces, cost tracking, replay mechanisms, and integration with monitoring platforms. It enables debugging, cost optimization, and compliance.

misconceptions.misconception: Persistence is only needed for long-running agents.
misconceptions.correction: Even short-lived agents benefit from checkpointing for crash recovery, debugging, and audit trails. Production systems require resume-from-checkpoint for reliability.

workedExamples.title: Tracing a single agent turn
workedExamples.prompt: An agent receives the task 'Check weather in Paris and send a summary email.' Trace the execution through one turn.
steps: Run: Agent starts with task and empty message history.
Turn 1: Assemble context (system prompt + task + tool schemas).
Step 1: Model returns tool_use for get_weather(location='Paris').
Tool dispatch: Validate request, execute get_weather, return result.
Turn 2: Assemble context (previous turn + tool result).
Step 2: Model returns tool_use for send_email(to='user', summary='Weather in Paris: 18°C, sunny').
Tool dispatch: HITL approval gate triggers for send_email.
User approves. Tool executes.
Turn 3: Model returns stop_reason='end_turn'.
Run completes.
workedExamples.explanation: Each turn assembles context, calls the model, dispatches tools, and checks stop conditions. The harness manages context assembly, tool validation, HITL gates, and loop termination.

workedExamples.title: Choosing a context assembly strategy
workedExamples.prompt: You're building a customer support agent that needs to reference conversation history. The average conversation is 50 turns. Your model has a 200k token context window but latency is a concern. Choose a context strategy.
steps: Evaluate full history: 50 turns × 500 tokens/turn = 25k tokens. Within budget but may increase latency.
Consider sliding window: Keep last 10 turns (5k tokens). Risk losing important context from earlier turns.
Consider summarization: Summarize turns older than 10. Balances history and token efficiency.
Decision: Use summarization for turns 1-40, full history for last 10 turns.
Implement: After turn 40, trigger summarization agent to compress old turns into 2k summary.
workedExamples.explanation: Context strategy depends on token budget, latency requirements, and task context needs. Summarization balances all three for long conversations.

workedExamples.title: Recovering from crash with checkpoints
workedExamples.prompt: An agent crashes after completing 8 of 10 steps in a workflow. Show how checkpointing enables recovery.
steps: Checkpoint format: { run_id, turn, step, messages[], state: { completed_steps: [...] } }
After step 8: Checkpoint saved with completed_steps: [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8].
Crash: Process terminates.
Recovery: Load checkpoint from storage.
Resume: Restore message history and state.
Continue from step 9: Skip completed steps 1-8.
Complete steps 9-10.
workedExamples.explanation: Checkpoints capture complete execution state (message history, agent state, step progress) to enable crash recovery without restarting from scratch.

exercises.prompt: Classify each of the following harness responsibilities into one of the eight concerns: (a) Deciding when to stop the loop, (b) Truncating old messages to fit token limits, (c) Saving state to disk after each turn, (d) Logging model calls to OpenTelemetry, (e) Asking user to approve a file deletion.
Design a tool dispatch system that validates tool calls against schemas, routes to implementations, handles timeouts, and formats errors. List the key components and their responsibilities.
You're building an agent that can read files, write files, and execute shell commands. Which actions should require human approval and why?

answers.answer: (a) Loop execution, (b) Context management, (c) Persistence, (d) Observability, (e) Human intervention

Components: (1) Schema validator: Validates tool_use against registered schemas. (2) Tool registry: Maps tool names to implementations. (3) Executor: Invokes tool with timeout wrapper. (4) Error handler: Catches exceptions, formats error messages for model. (5) Result formatter: Converts tool output to tool_result format.

Require approval for: (1) Write file: Prevents unintended data loss. (2) Execute shell commands: Prevents destructive actions (rm -rf, etc.). (3) Optional for read file: Depends on sensitivity of data. Rationale: Consequential, irreversible, or security-sensitive actions should require explicit user consent.
rubric: Identifies write and execute as requiring approval
Provides reasonable rationale about consequences or risk

formativeAssessment.prompt: What are the eight operational concerns of an agent harness?
formativeAssessment.expectedAnswer: Loop execution, tool dispatch, context management, state and goals, control flow, persistence, observability, and human intervention.

formativeAssessment.prompt: Classify harness responsibilities into the correct concern category.
formativeAssessment.expectedAnswer: Learner correctly classifies at least 7 out of 8 sample responsibilities.

formativeAssessment.prompt: Trace a single agent turn from context assembly through tool dispatch to stop condition checking.
formativeAssessment.expectedAnswer: Describes context assembly → model call → tool dispatch → result integration → stop check cycle.

formativeAssessment.prompt: Review this tool dispatch implementation and identify missing components.
formativeAssessment.expectedAnswer: Identifies missing schema validation, error handling, or timeout mechanisms.

formativeAssessment.prompt: Design a context assembly strategy for a 100-turn conversation within a 50k token budget.
formativeAssessment.expectedAnswer: Proposes sliding window, summarization, or hybrid strategy with rationale.

formativeAssessment.prompt: Implement a checkpoint save/restore mechanism for an agent run.
formativeAssessment.expectedAnswer: Implementation includes run_id, turn, step, messages, and state fields, with save and load functions.

formativeAssessment.prompt: Design an approval gate system for a production agent with file system access.
formativeAssessment.expectedAnswer: Identifies consequential actions (write, delete, execute), proposes approval mechanism, includes bypass for safe reads.

formativeAssessment.prompt: Submit your minimal harness implementation with test suite.
formativeAssessment.expectedAnswer: Working harness with loop execution, tool dispatch, context management, and passing tests using FakeModelClient.

formativeAssessment.prompt: Compare LangGraph and Temporal across the eight harness concerns.
formativeAssessment.expectedAnswer: Matrix comparing both frameworks on all eight concerns with accurate characterizations.

masteryCriteria.description: Can define the harness layer and accurately classify its eight operational concerns.
masteryCriteria.threshold: Correctly identifies all eight concerns and classifies at least 7 of 8 sample responsibilities.

masteryCriteria.description: Can trace agent execution through run/turn/step model with correct sequencing.
masteryCriteria.threshold: Accurately traces a multi-turn execution including context assembly, tool dispatch, and stop conditions.

masteryCriteria.description: Can implement a complete tool dispatch system with validation and error handling.
masteryCriteria.threshold: Implementation includes schema validation, registry, timeout handling, and error formatting.

masteryCriteria.description: Can design context assembly strategies that balance history, token limits, and latency.
masteryCriteria.threshold: Proposes appropriate strategy for given scenario with clear rationale.

masteryCriteria.description: Can implement checkpoint-based persistence enabling crash recovery.
masteryCriteria.threshold: Implementation supports save, load, and resume with complete state capture.

masteryCriteria.description: Can design human-in-the-loop patterns appropriate for action consequences.
masteryCriteria.threshold: Identifies consequential actions requiring approval with sound risk assessment.

masteryCriteria.description: Can build a functional agent harness from first principles.
masteryCriteria.threshold: Working harness passes all test cases with deterministic model stub.

masteryCriteria.description: Can evaluate production frameworks against the eight-concern framework.
masteryCriteria.threshold: Accurately compares at least two frameworks on all eight concerns.

recap: The harness layer manages eight operational concerns: loop execution, tool dispatch, context management, state tracking, control flow, persistence, observability, and human intervention.
Production harnesses require checkpoint-based persistence, structured observability, and human-in-the-loop gates for consequential actions.
Framework choice depends on your concern priorities: LangGraph for graph-based control flow, Temporal for durable execution, custom for maximum control.
nextSteps: Complete the hands-on labs to build and extend a harness from scratch.
Evaluate production frameworks against the eight-concern framework for your use case.
Integrate MCP tools into your harness for ecosystem interoperability.
Instrument your harness with OpenTelemetry for production observability.

culturalAssumptions: Examples assume familiarity with Python and command-line development

pointRules.description: Bonus points for perfect quiz score.
Extra points for completing hands-on labs.

adaptivePaths.title: Conceptual Fast Track
adaptivePaths.description: Cover the eight harness concerns and decision framework without hands-on labs.

adaptivePaths.title: Full Build Path
adaptivePaths.description: Complete all 12 lessons including hands-on labs and ecosystem analysis.

projectCapstones.title: Build a Production-Ready Harness
projectCapstones.description: Build a complete agent harness implementing all eight concerns with comprehensive test coverage.
deliverables: Working harness with all eight concerns implemented
Test suite with FakeModelClient for deterministic testing
Architecture decision document explaining design choices
Integration with at least one MCP tool server
rubric: Implements all eight harness concerns correctly
Includes checkpoint-based persistence with crash recovery
Has structured trace output compatible with OpenTelemetry
Passes all tests with deterministic model stub
